stem: A quantity \( P(t) \) obeys \( \frac{dP}{dt} = kP \) and multiplies by a factor of [[f]] every [[T]] hours. If \( P(0) = [[P0]] \), what is \( P([[t]]) \) (with \( t \) in hours)?
solution: Because \( P \) multiplies by [[f]] every [[T]] hours, in [[t]] hours there are \( [[t]]/[[T]] = [[n]] \) such intervals. Thus \( P([[t]]) = [[P0]]\cdot[[f]]^{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This grows linearly, multiplying by [[f]] times the number of intervals instead of raising [[f]] to that power.
This uses one too few doubling intervals.
This uses one too many doubling intervals.
This swaps the base and the exponent.
This adds the growth factor instead of multiplying by it.
This adds one extra copy of the initial amount.
This doubles the correct result.

stem: A quantity satisfies \( \dfrac{dP}{dt} = [[k]]P \) with \( P(0) = [[P0]] \). What is \( P([[t]]) \)?
solution: The general solution of \( P' = [[k]]P \) is \( P(t) = P(0)e^{[[k]]t} \). With \( P(0) = [[P0]] \), \( P([[t]]) = [[P0]]\,e^{[[k]]\cdot[[t]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This flips the sign of the exponent, giving decay instead of growth (or vice versa).
This grows linearly in \( kt \) instead of exponentially.
This adds the exponential term instead of multiplying by it.
This multiplies by \( t \) after exponentiating instead of putting \( t \) in the exponent.
This uses one fewer unit of time in the exponent.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.

stem: A quantity \( P(t) \) obeys \( \frac{dP}{dt} = kP \) and multiplies by a factor of [[f]] every [[T]] hours. If \( P(0) = [[P0]] \), after how many [[T]]-hour periods does the quantity first reach \( [[Pt]] \)?
solution: Since \( P \) multiplies by [[f]] each period, \( [[P0]] \cdot [[f]]^{n} = [[Pt]] \); comparing exponents (or taking logs) gives \( n = [[answer]] \) periods.
distractor_rationales: This uses one period too many.
This uses one period too few.
This uses two periods too many.
This reports the raw ratio \( P_t/P_0 \) instead of solving \( f^n \) equal to that ratio.
This multiplies the growth factor by \( n \) instead of exponentiating.
This squares the correct number of periods.
This confuses the number of periods with the elapsed time in hours.

stem: A quantity satisfies \( \dfrac{dP}{dt} = kP \) for a constant rate \( k \). If \( P([[t1]]) = e^{[[j]]}\,P(0) \), what is \( k \)?
solution: Since \( P(t) = P(0)e^{kt} \), the condition \( P([[t1]]) = e^{[[j]]}P(0) \) means \( e^{k\cdot[[t1]]} = e^{[[j]]} \), so \( k\cdot[[t1]] = [[j]] \) and \( k = \dfrac{[[j]]}{[[t1]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio, dividing time by the exponent instead of the exponent by time.
This multiplies the exponent by the time instead of dividing.
This has the wrong sign on \( k \).
This uses one more than the correct exponent.
This uses one more than the correct elapsed time in the denominator.
This is one more than the correct rate.
This is one less than the correct rate.
